items.title: Customer lifetime value from order history
items.scenario: An e-commerce team has `orders` (one row per order) and needs `total_spend` and `order_count` per `customer_id` for a churn model.
items.explanation: Use `orders.groupby('customer_id').agg(total_spend=('amount', 'sum'), order_count=('order_id', 'size'))`, then `merge` the summary onto a customer-level base table with `how='left'` so customers with zero orders remain as NaN or zero-filled features.

items.title: Enriching events with product category
items.scenario: Clickstream logs have `product_id`; a separate `products` table maps `product_id` → `category`. Analysts need category on each click row.
items.explanation: Left merge clicks to products on `product_id`. Unmatched IDs (delisted products) keep click rows with NaN category—prefer left over inner so you do not drop traffic silently.

items.title: Leaky user-level average before train/test split
items.scenario: A notebook computes `df['user_mean_score'] = df.groupby('user_id')['score'].transform('mean')` on all rows, then splits 80/20. Validation AUC looks strong.
items.explanation: Test rows contributed to their user's mean, leaking test information into features. Fix: split by user (or time) first, compute means on train users only, map to test with train-learned stats or out-of-fold encoding.

items.title: Many-to-many merge inflates training rows
items.scenario: Merging `patients` to `diagnoses` on `hospital_id` (not patient key) because both tables share it. Row count jumps from 10k to 400k.
items.explanation: Keys were not unique on one side—each patient paired with every diagnosis at that hospital. Use patient-level keys, `validate='m:1'` or `'1:1'`, and `indicator=True` to catch unintended cartesian expansion before modeling.
